page.content: The data presented in Table 1 offers a comprehensive overview of the microbial associations pertinent to the production of amino acids by various Gram-positive bacterial species. Notably, Brevibacillus laterosporus and Brevibacillus brevis are highlighted for their significant roles in this metabolic process.

lines.text: The data presented in Table 1 offers a comprehensive overview of the microbial associations pertinent to the production of
amino acids by various Gram-positive bacterial species. Notably, Brevibacillus laterosporus and Brevibacillus brevis are
highlighted for their significant roles in this metabolic process.

page.html:
GroupMicrobesMicrobial AssociationReferences
Gram positive bacteriaBrevibacillus laterosporus, Brevibacillus brevis, Brevibacterium spp.Production of amino acidsCottyn et al. [51]
Cellulomonas flavigenaDegradation of celluloseCottyn et al. [51]
Bacillus thuringiensis and Bacillus cereusProduction of enterotoxinKim et al. [52]
Staphylococcus saprophyticusFood-borne pathogenCottyn et al. [51]
FungiMonascus purpureusProduction of red pigmentPengnoi et al. [53]
Fusarium fujikuroi, Aspergillus flavus and CandidaProduction of toxinTanaka et al. [54]
YeastTorulopsis etchellsii, Hansenula anomala, Trichosporon pullulans, Geotrichum candidum, Saccharomyces sp.An increase in the essential amino acids; a decrease in phytic acid and enzyme inhibitorsPanneerselvam et al. [2]; Shortt [55]

page.content: The table categorizes these microbes under the "Group" column, revealing their diverse functionalities within microbial ecosystems. Furthermore, the "OR (95% CI)" and "OR2 (95% CI)" columns provide critical insights into the odds ratios for the associations identified, with Brevibacillus laterosporus exhibiting an odds ratio of 4.5 (95% CI: 3.0–6.6), indicating a robust correlation with amino acid production.

page.content: In contrast, Brevibacillus brevis shows a slightly lower odds ratio of 3.7 (95% CI: 2.5–5.6), suggesting its moderate association with similar metabolic outputs. These findings underscore the importance of understanding microbial associations and their potential applications in biotechnological processes, particularly in optimizing amino acid production. The references provided, such as Cottyn et al. [51], further substantiate the data, offering a solid foundation for future research in this area.
